content.text: Sampling strategies are the set of algorithms that decide how to draw the next token from the probability distribution produced by a language model. After the model computes a distribution P(xₜ₊₁ | context) over its vocabulary, sampling is the step that converts this distribution into an actual token choice. The choice is not trivial: sampling directly from the raw distribution often produces degenerate outputs — either too repetitive (always picking the most likely token) or too incoherent (sampling uniformly at random). Sampling strategies are the engineering layer between the model's learned distribution and the user-visible output.

The three foundational parameters are temperature, top-k, and top-p (nucleus sampling). They are not mutually exclusive — real production systems apply them in combination: typically top-k first to eliminate extreme outliers, then nucleus sampling to dynamically size the effective vocabulary, then temperature to calibrate confidence, then final multinomial sampling. Understanding each parameter independently and in combination is essential for controlling LLM generation quality.

These parameters only apply at inference time — they have no effect on training. They manipulate the logit vector or the resulting probability distribution immediately before sampling, and different settings can produce radically different outputs from the exact same model checkpoint. The right settings depend entirely on your task: creative writing demands different values than code generation or factual Q&A.

Sampling strategy is one of the highest-leverage, zero-cost levers in LLM engineering. It requires no fine-tuning, no training compute, and no additional infrastructure — changing a single float parameter can shift outputs from repetitive boilerplate to creative prose to incoherent noise. For agentic AI systems where the model drives multi-step plans, sampling strategy affects whether the agent explores alternative approaches or locks into a single path.

Poor sampling choices are responsible for a large fraction of visible LLM quality failures in production. Greedy decoding (temperature=0) causes repetition loops in open-ended generation. High temperature on structured tasks like JSON generation or code synthesis causes invalid syntax. Overly narrow nucleus (low top-p) on brainstorming tasks produces repetitive, unimaginative outputs. Overly wide nucleus on factual tasks produces hallucinated tokens that were low-probability but not impossible.

For multi-step agentic tasks where the model must produce tool calls, plans, or structured outputs, sampling strategy is often the deciding factor between a working pipeline and a broken one. JSON schema-guided decoding and logit masking (which force certain tokens to zero probability) are extensions of the sampling concept that constrain generation to valid structured formats.

Think of the probability distribution as a probability dial over thousands of tokens. At each inference step, the model sets this dial — some tokens are very likely, many are moderately likely, and a long tail is very unlikely but not impossible. Sampling strategies are different ways of reading that dial: you can always pick the highest value (greedy), spin it with equal weight on all positions (pure random sampling), or bias it to concentrate more on high values (temperature scaling) or to exclude the low tail entirely (top-k / top-p).

A useful physical analogy: temperature is borrowed from statistical mechanics, where temperature controls the energy distribution of particles. At T→0, all particles settle into the ground state (minimum energy, maximum order). At T→∞, particles are distributed uniformly across all energy states (maximum disorder). For LLMs: temperature near 0 makes the model always pick its most confident token (deterministic, repetitive), temperature near infinity produces uniform random token selection (incoherent). The operating range for useful generation is roughly 0.5–1.5.

Top-k and top-p (nucleus) are vocabulary truncation strategies. They address a fundamental problem with the raw distribution: the long tail of improbable tokens. Even a token with probability 0.0001 will occasionally be sampled when generating millions of tokens. If that token is grammatically incoherent or factually absurd, it degrades the output. Truncating the tail prevents these low-probability disasters. Top-k cuts a fixed number of tokens; top-p cuts dynamically based on how concentrated or spread-out the distribution is.
Adjusting temperature and nucleus parameters requires no retraining, fine-tuning, or infrastructure changes. A single parameter change can dramatically improve output quality for a given task.

Factual tasks benefit from low temperature and narrow nucleus. Creative tasks benefit from higher temperature. Code generation benefits from very low temperature plus structured decoding. The same model serves all tasks by adjusting these parameters.

Sampling with temperature > 0 allows generating multiple diverse candidates for the same prompt, which enables self-consistency (voting across samples), best-of-N selection, and chain-of-thought verification.

The combination of top-p + moderate temperature achieves a useful middle ground: creative variation (through stochasticity) without incoherence (through tail truncation). This was a significant advance over pure temperature sampling.

Repetition penalties, frequency penalties, and presence penalties are extensions of logit manipulation that reduce the probability of tokens that have already appeared, directly addressing one of the most common generation failure modes.

Any temperature > 0 introduces non-determinism. Two runs with the same prompt and parameters can produce different outputs. For testing, debugging, and evaluation, this requires fixed random seeds and multiple evaluation runs.

Lower temperature and narrower nucleus produce more consistent, higher-quality outputs for well-defined tasks but reduce diversity. Higher temperature and wider nucleus produce more varied outputs but increase the rate of errors and incoherence.
There is no universally optimal temperature — the right value depends on the model, the task, and the desired output style. Values need to be calibrated per deployment.

Top-k always keeps exactly k tokens regardless of whether the distribution is sharply peaked (where 3 tokens capture 99% of probability) or flat (where 50 tokens share 99%). This makes it less adaptive than nucleus sampling.

Temperature, top-k, and top-p interact multiplicatively. Setting all three without understanding their interaction can produce unexpected behavior. The standard order of operations is: apply top-k → apply top-p → apply temperature → sample.

At temperature=0 (greedy decoding), the model can enter stable repetition loops where the most likely next token given the previous generated text is to repeat the previous phrase indefinitely. This is a known failure mode with no elegant fix except min-p or repetition penalties.

Temperature T rescales the logits before the softmax. The raw logits lᵢ (real numbers, one per vocabulary token) are divided by T: P(xᵢ) = softmax(l/T)ᵢ. When T < 1, dividing by T makes the logits larger in magnitude, which sharpens the softmax distribution toward the maximum (lower entropy, more peaked). When T > 1, dividing makes logits smaller, flattening the distribution (higher entropy, more uniform). At T = 1, no change occurs — this is the model's native distribution.

The effect on entropy: at T → 0, the distribution collapses to a point mass on the argmax (greedy decoding). At T → ∞, the distribution approaches uniform (1/V for each token). In between, temperature provides smooth interpolation between these extremes.

Top-k sampling keeps only the k highest-probability tokens and assigns zero probability to all others, then renormalizes. Formally: given sorted probabilities p₁ ≥ p₂ ≥ ... ≥ pₙ, keep tokens 1..k, set p_{k+1}..pₙ = 0, and divide by Σᵢ₌₁ᵏ pᵢ. This prevents any token outside the top-k from ever being sampled, eliminating the long tail at the cost of fixed vocabulary size regardless of the distribution's shape. Common values: k=40–100 for chat, k=5–20 for code.

Top-p (nucleus) sampling dynamically selects the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Formally: sort tokens by probability descending, then include tokens until their cumulative probability ≥ p. Renormalize those tokens to sum to 1, sample from them. When the distribution is peaked (one token has probability 0.95), the nucleus contains only that token (very conservative). When the distribution is flat (many tokens share similar probability), the nucleus expands to include more tokens (more diverse). This adaptive behavior is the key advantage over fixed top-k.

min-p (minimum probability threshold) is a newer strategy that removes any token whose absolute probability is below a minimum fraction of the most probable token's probability. If the most probable token has probability p_max, then all tokens with probability < min_p × p_max are removed. At min_p=0.1 and p_max=0.8, tokens with probability below 0.08 are excluded. This scales the cutoff with the distribution's confidence, addressing top-k's fixed-k problem and nucleus's behavior at extreme p values.

In production, these are typically applied in sequence: (1) compute logits from the model, (2) apply repetition/frequency penalties (logit subtraction), (3) apply top-k to eliminate extreme low-probability tokens, (4) apply top-p to dynamically size the remaining nucleus, (5) apply temperature scaling to the remaining logits, (6) softmax to get probabilities, (7) multinomial sample. Different libraries implement slightly different orderings — check the source code of the library you are using.
The diagram below illustrates how each sampling strategy reshapes the probability distribution before the final token selection step.

attrs.alt: Sampling strategies visualization: temperature sharpening/flattening, top-k truncation, and nucleus (top-p) dynamic cutoff illustrated as bar charts

content.text: The critical insight in the top-p panel: the nucleus adapts its size to the distribution. When the model is confident (highly peaked), the nucleus is small — perhaps 1-3 tokens. When the model is uncertain (flat distribution), the nucleus expands to 20-50 tokens. Top-k cannot achieve this adaptive behavior since it always keeps exactly k tokens.
Accuracy over diversity; greedy often best
Syntax correctness critical; low temp + schema guided
Tool names and args must be exact; very low temp

For self-consistency (running the same prompt multiple times and voting on the answer), use temperature in the 0.5–0.8 range to generate diversity across samples while keeping each individual sample relatively coherent.
The following implements all core sampling strategies from scratch, operating directly on logit tensors:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from typing import Optional

def apply_temperature(logits: torch.Tensor, temperature: float) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Scale logits by temperature. temperature=1.0 is no-op."""
    if temperature == 0.0:
        # Return one-hot at the argmax (greedy)
        one_hot = torch.zeros_like(logits)
        one_hot.scatter_(-1, logits.argmax(dim=-1, keepdim=True), 1.0)
        return one_hot
    return logits / temperature


def apply_top_k(logits: torch.Tensor, k: int) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Zero out all logits except the top-k. Returns modified logits."""
    if k <= 0 or k >= logits.size(-1):
        return logits
    # Find the k-th largest value
    top_k_values, _ = torch.topk(logits, k, dim=-1)
    kth_value = top_k_values[..., -1:].expand_as(logits)
    # Mask positions below the k-th value to -inf
    return logits.masked_fill(logits < kth_value, float("-inf"))


def apply_top_p(logits: torch.Tensor, p: float) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Zero out tokens outside the nucleus (smallest set summing to >= p)."""
    if p >= 1.0:
        return logits
    # Sort in descending order
    sorted_logits, sorted_indices = torch.sort(logits, dim=-1, descending=True)
    cumulative_probs = sorted_logits.softmax(dim=-1).cumsum(dim=-1)
    # Identify positions beyond the nucleus: cumul_prob > p AFTER the token
    # We shift right so token whose cumul prob crosses p is still included
    sorted_to_remove = cumulative_probs - sorted_logits.softmax(dim=-1) >= p
    # Scatter back to original ordering
    indices_to_remove = sorted_to_remove.scatter(-1, sorted_indices, sorted_to_remove)
    return logits.masked_fill(indices_to_remove, float("-inf"))


def apply_min_p(logits: torch.Tensor, min_p: float) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Remove tokens whose probability < min_p * max_token_probability."""
    probs = logits.softmax(dim=-1)
    max_prob = probs.max(dim=-1, keepdim=True).values
    threshold = min_p * max_prob
    return logits.masked_fill(probs < threshold, float("-inf"))


def apply_repetition_penalty(
    logits: torch.Tensor,
    input_ids: torch.Tensor,
    penalty: float = 1.0
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Multiplicative repetition penalty: divide logits of previously seen tokens
    by penalty if logit is positive, multiply if negative.
    penalty > 1.0 penalizes repetition; < 1.0 encourages it.
    """
    if penalty == 1.0:
        return logits
    score = logits.gather(-1, input_ids)
    score = torch.where(score < 0, score * penalty, score / penalty)
    return logits.scatter(-1, input_ids, score)


def sample_next_token(
    logits: torch.Tensor,            # (vocab_size,) or (batch, vocab_size)
    temperature: float = 1.0,
    top_k: int = 0,
    top_p: float = 1.0,
    min_p: float = 0.0,
    repetition_penalty: float = 1.0,
    input_ids: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Full sampling pipeline: penalties → top-k → top-p → min-p → temperature → sample.
    Returns the sampled token ID(s).
    """
    # Ensure (batch, vocab) shape
    if logits.dim() == 1:
        logits = logits.unsqueeze(0)
        squeeze = True
    else:
        squeeze = False

    # Step 1: Repetition penalty
    if repetition_penalty != 1.0 and input_ids is not None:
        logits = apply_repetition_penalty(logits, input_ids, repetition_penalty)

    # Step 2: Top-k
    if top_k > 0:
        logits = apply_top_k(logits, top_k)

    # Step 3: Top-p (nucleus)
    if top_p < 1.0:
        logits = apply_top_p(logits, top_p)

    # Step 4: min-p
    if min_p > 0.0:
        logits = apply_min_p(logits, min_p)

    # Step 5: Temperature
    logits = apply_temperature(logits, temperature)

    # Step 6: Convert to probabilities and sample
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
    # Replace any NaN (from all-inf logits) with uniform
    probs = torch.nan_to_num(probs, nan=1.0 / probs.size(-1))
    token = torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)

    return token.squeeze(0) if squeeze else token


# ── Quick test ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if __name__ == "__main__":
    torch.manual_seed(42)
    # Simulate logits for a vocabulary of 100 tokens
    vocab_size = 100
    logits = torch.randn(vocab_size)
    # Make tokens 5, 10, 20 much more likely
    logits[[5, 10, 20]] += 5.0

    print("Greedy (temperature=0):")
    print(" Token:", sample_next_token(logits, temperature=0.0).item())

    print("\nTop-k=5, temperature=0.7:")
    for _ in range(5):
        tok = sample_next_token(logits, temperature=0.7, top_k=5).item()
        print(f"  token {tok}")

    print("\nNucleus p=0.9, temperature=1.0:")
    for _ in range(5):
        tok = sample_next_token(logits, temperature=1.0, top_p=0.9).item()
        print(f"  token {tok}")
This script generates text under different sampling configurations and prints them side by side for comparison, making it easy to understand the effect of each parameter empirically:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

model_name = "gpt2"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name).eval()

prompt = "The future of artificial intelligence is"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

configs = [
    {"name": "Greedy",               "do_sample": False, "temperature": 1.0},
    {"name": "Temp=0.3 (sharp)",     "do_sample": True,  "temperature": 0.3, "top_k": 0, "top_p": 1.0},
    {"name": "Temp=1.0 (neutral)",   "do_sample": True,  "temperature": 1.0, "top_k": 0, "top_p": 1.0},
    {"name": "Temp=1.5 (creative)",  "do_sample": True,  "temperature": 1.5, "top_k": 0, "top_p": 1.0},
    {"name": "Top-k=5",              "do_sample": True,  "temperature": 1.0, "top_k": 5, "top_p": 1.0},
    {"name": "Top-p=0.85",           "do_sample": True,  "temperature": 1.0, "top_k": 0, "top_p": 0.85},
    {"name": "Top-k=50, top-p=0.9",  "do_sample": True,  "temperature": 0.9, "top_k": 50, "top_p": 0.9},
]

torch.manual_seed(123)
for cfg in configs:
    name = cfg.pop("name")
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=40, **cfg)
    text = tokenizer.decode(out[0][inputs["input_ids"].size(1):], skip_special_tokens=True)
    print(f"[{name}]")
    print(f"  {text.strip()[:120]}")
    print()

Self-consistency (Wang et al., 2022) generates multiple reasoning chains at moderate temperature and votes on the majority answer. This is one of the most effective prompting techniques for math and reasoning tasks:

from transformers import pipeline
from collections import Counter
import re, torch

# Use any instruction-tuned model; gpt2 is just for demonstration
gen = pipeline("text-generation", model="gpt2", device=-1)

def extract_answer(text: str) -> str:
    """Extract a number from a chain-of-thought answer."""
    matches = re.findall(r"\b\d+\b", text)
    return matches[-1] if matches else "?"

prompt = "Q: A store has 24 apples. It sells 7 and gets 15 more. How many apples? A: Let me think step by step."

# Generate N diverse reasoning chains
N = 8
torch.manual_seed(0)
answers = []
for i in range(N):
    output = gen(
        prompt,
        max_new_tokens=80,
        do_sample=True,
        temperature=0.7,   # moderate temperature for diverse but coherent chains
        top_p=0.9,
        return_full_text=False,
    )
    completion = output[0]["generated_text"]
    ans = extract_answer(completion)
    answers.append(ans)
    print(f"Chain {i+1}: ...{completion[-60:].strip()!r} → answer={ans}")

# Majority vote
counter = Counter(answers)
majority_answer, majority_count = counter.most_common(1)[0]
print(f"\nSelf-consistency vote: {counter}")
print(f"Final answer: {majority_answer} (from {majority_count}/{N} chains)")
# Correct answer: 24 - 7 + 15 = 32
Logit bias directly modifies the probability of specific tokens, enabling explicit control over what the model can or cannot generate. This is the building block for format-constrained generation:

# Note: logit_bias is available in the OpenAI API and some local inference engines.
# This example shows the concept using raw logit manipulation in HuggingFace.

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

# Suppose we want the model to only output one of: "Yes", "No", "Maybe"
allowed_words = ["Yes", " Yes", "No", " No", "Maybe", " Maybe"]
allowed_token_ids = []
for word in allowed_words:
    ids = tokenizer.encode(word, add_special_tokens=False)
    if len(ids) == 1:  # only single-token words for simplicity
        allowed_token_ids.extend(ids)

prompt = "Is Python a good language for machine learning? Answer with Yes, No, or Maybe:"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

logits = outputs.logits[0, -1, :].clone()  # (vocab_size,)

# Zero out all tokens except allowed
mask = torch.full_like(logits, float("-inf"))
mask[allowed_token_ids] = 0.0
constrained_logits = logits + mask

probs = constrained_logits.softmax(dim=-1)
print("Constrained next-token probabilities:")
for tok_id in set(allowed_token_ids):
    token_str = tokenizer.decode([tok_id])
    print(f"  {repr(token_str)}: {probs[tok_id].item():.4f}")

OpenAI's API exposes all major sampling parameters: temperature, top_p, frequency_penalty, and presence_penalty. A production chatbot for customer support might use the following settings to balance accuracy with natural conversational tone:

# Production chatbot configuration (illustrative — uses OpenAI API structure)
import json

PRODUCTION_CHAT_CONFIG = {
    # Core sampling parameters
    "temperature": 0.4,       # Low enough for consistent, accurate responses
    "top_p": 0.85,            # Dynamic nucleus; prevents wild token sampling
    "max_tokens": 512,        # Cap response length

    # Repetition control
    "frequency_penalty": 0.3, # Penalize tokens proportional to how often they've appeared
    "presence_penalty": 0.1,  # Penalize tokens that have appeared at all (encourages topic diversity)

    # Structured output
    "response_format": {"type": "json_object"},  # Force JSON output mode
}

# For internal reasoning/chain-of-thought steps (not user-facing):
REASONING_CONFIG = {
    "temperature": 0.6,       # Higher for exploratory reasoning
    "top_p": 0.9,
    "max_tokens": 2000,       # Long chain-of-thought
    "frequency_penalty": 0.0,
    "presence_penalty": 0.0,
}

# For code generation:
CODE_GEN_CONFIG = {
    "temperature": 0.1,       # Very deterministic — syntax must be correct
    "top_p": 0.5,             # Narrow nucleus
    "max_tokens": 1024,
    "stop": ["```", "\n\n\n"],  # Stop at code block boundaries
}

print(json.dumps({"chat": PRODUCTION_CHAT_CONFIG,
                  "reasoning": REASONING_CONFIG,
                  "code": CODE_GEN_CONFIG}, indent=2))

The Anthropic Claude API uses a temperature parameter (0–1 range by default) and the internal implementation uses a combination of temperature, top-k, and top-p that varies by model. For production deployments, starting with temperature=1.0 (the model's default) and adjusting based on observed output quality is recommended over guessing initial values.
Mistake 1 — Using temperature=0 for all tasks

Temperature=0 (greedy decoding) is often misused as a 'safe default' for all tasks. While it maximizes predictability, it causes repetition loops in open-ended generation and prevents exploring alternative valid continuations. The right default depends on the task; many conversational applications work best at temperature=0.7–1.0.

# PROBLEMATIC: greedy decoding for open-ended generation
# Often produces: "The quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog. The quick brown fox..."
outputs = model.generate(inputs, max_new_tokens=200, do_sample=False)  # temperature=0

# BETTER: moderate sampling for open-ended generation
outputs = model.generate(
    inputs, max_new_tokens=200,
    do_sample=True, temperature=0.8, top_p=0.9, top_k=50
)
Mistake 2 — Setting top_p=1.0 with high temperature

High temperature alone flattens the distribution, giving meaningful probability to grammatically incorrect, factually wrong, or semantically incoherent tokens. Without a nucleus constraint, these tokens are sampled with growing frequency. Always pair high temperature with a nucleus constraint:

# PROBLEMATIC: high temperature + no nucleus → incoherent outputs
outputs = model.generate(inputs, max_new_tokens=100, do_sample=True,
                         temperature=1.5, top_p=1.0)  # no truncation at all

# BETTER: high temperature + nucleus to prevent worst-case tokens
outputs = model.generate(inputs, max_new_tokens=100, do_sample=True,
                         temperature=1.2, top_p=0.95, top_k=100)
Mistake 3 — Applying top-p before top-k in the wrong order

The order of top-k and top-p matters. Applying top-p first when the distribution is flat may include many tokens, then top-k may reduce to a different set than intended. The conventional order is: top-k first (hard cutoff), then top-p (dynamic nucleus from remaining), then temperature. Different libraries may implement different orders — always check documentation.
Start from the model's recommended defaults:
Model cards and API documentation often include recommended sampling parameters calibrated to the model's training distribution. These are better starting points than universal defaults.
Greedy decoding is deterministic and reproducible, making it useful for evaluations and comparisons. For production use, prefer sampling to avoid repetition loops.
Combine top-k=50 + top-p=0.9 as your baseline:
This combination is the most widely-used heuristic for general text generation. Top-k=50 eliminates extreme outliers; top-p=0.9 handles varying distribution shapes.
Calibrate temperature per task with human evaluation:
Run A/B tests with different temperatures on actual user prompts and measure preference. The optimal value is task-dependent and user-dependent.
Use logit bias for format enforcement:
When you need strict output formats (single letter answers, specific keywords, JSON keys), logit bias is cleaner than post-processing. Set allowed tokens to 0 bias and all others to -100.
Apply repetition penalty for long generations:

For outputs longer than ~200 tokens, frequency_penalty=0.1–0.3 significantly reduces repetitive phrases and loops. Too high a penalty (>1.5) causes the model to avoid necessary repeated terms (like function names in code).
When debugging generation quality issues, the sampling configuration is as important as the prompt. Log both together.

Sampling itself has negligible computational cost compared to the Transformer forward pass. The logit manipulation (top-k sort, top-p cumsum) operates on a vector of size V (vocabulary size, typically 32k–200k) and takes microseconds on GPU. The sampling step (multinomial) is similarly fast.

The main performance consideration is batch diversity vs. batch uniformity. In batched inference, all sequences in a batch must run for the same number of steps (or be padded). High-temperature sampling tends to produce shorter or longer completions than greedy decoding, requiring more dynamic batching or early stopping management. vLLM's PagedAttention handles this by decoupling KV cache allocation from batch positions.

Speculative decoding, which requires a small draft model to generate k tokens, is sensitive to sampling configuration: the draft and target models must use compatible sampling parameters for the acceptance/rejection step to preserve the target distribution. Most implementations of speculative decoding operate at temperature=1.0 internally and apply final temperature scaling at the acceptance step.

Implements all standard sampling strategies plus beam search, constrained beam search, and diverse beam search. Parameters: do_sample, temperature, top_k, top_p, repetition_penalty, min_length, max_new_tokens.

Production-grade inference server. SamplingParams(temperature=0.8, top_p=0.95, max_tokens=512) is the main configuration interface. Supports logprobs output, token-level streaming, and best-of-N sampling.
Local inference for quantized models. Exposes temperature, top_k, top_p, min_p, and typical-p (another adaptive sampling strategy). CLI: llama-cli -m model.gguf --temp 0.8 --top-p 0.9
temperature and top_p are the main exposed parameters. frequency_penalty and presence_penalty (OpenAI) provide repetition control. top_k is exposed in some APIs.
Structured generation library that constrains sampling to valid tokens for a given JSON schema, regex, or grammar. Implements logit masking at each step for guaranteed structured output.
Allows interleaving Python control flow with LLM generation, enabling fine-grained control over what tokens can be sampled at each position.
Creative writing at T>1; factual at T<1
Incoherence at high T with no nucleus
Too restrictive when distribution is flat
Very flat distributions may include too many tokens
Mode collapse; all beams converge to similar text
Grammar must cover all valid outputs

High-temperature sampling increases the probability of sampling rare tokens, including tokens that appear infrequently in training data (and may correlate with sensitive or unusual content). For systems that require consistent, predictable outputs, lower temperature reduces the variance of rare events. For systems with strict content filters, sampling entropy directly affects how often the filter needs to intervene.

Very low temperature (near-greedy) makes the model's behavior more predictable to adversaries. If an attacker can observe output distributions, they can use greedy behavior to more reliably predict model responses to variations of the same prompt, potentially enabling systematic adversarial probing. Some production systems intentionally add small temperature noise even for tasks that nominally want deterministic outputs.

Repetition penalties that are too strong can prevent the model from outputting tokens that genuinely need to repeat — variable names in code, proper nouns in factual text, formula terms in mathematical explanations. Always evaluate repetition penalty on domain-specific content, not just general text.
Temperature scales logits before softmax: T < 1 sharpens the distribution (more confident, more repetitive), T > 1 flattens it (more diverse, more random), T = 1 is the model's native distribution.

Top-k keeps exactly k tokens and zeros the rest — simple but not adaptive. Top-p (nucleus) keeps the smallest set summing to ≥ p cumulative probability — adaptive to how peaked or flat the distribution is.
The conventional application order is: repetition penalties → top-k → top-p → temperature → softmax → multinomial sample. Different libraries may vary; always check implementation.

The right sampling configuration is task-dependent: code generation and structured output demand near-greedy (low temperature, narrow nucleus); creative writing benefits from higher temperature and wider nucleus.
Self-consistency (generate N samples at moderate temperature, majority vote) is one of the highest-value use cases for non-greedy sampling — it consistently improves accuracy on reasoning tasks.

Logit bias / logit masking enables constrained generation by setting specific token logits to -inf (to ban) or large positive values (to force). This is the foundation of grammar-guided and schema-constrained decoding.
Greedy decoding (temperature=0) is deterministic and reproducible but prone to repetition loops in open-ended generation. Never use it as a general default without testing for repetition behavior.

Sampling parameters have zero training cost — they operate only at inference time. Calibrating them through human evaluation is one of the cheapest quality improvements available for any LLM deployment.
